items.title: Morgan Stanley’s Joseph Moore said that, after speaking with several purchasing contacts in the data center space last week, the intensity of the memory shortages shows no signs of abating. He added that prices appear to be up at least 25% on a like-for-like basis from 2Q to 3Q. This is above both Morgan Stanley’s and third-party estimates. Moore also noted that longer-term concerns that the memory shortage will intensify in 2027 and again in 2028 remain as strong as ever. Morgan Stanley added that there is not enough memory relative to AI requirements and that it does not see this situation changing. items.content:
Morgan Stanley’s Joseph Moore said that, after speaking with several purchasing contacts in the data center space last week, the intensity of the memory shortages shows no signs of abating. He added that prices appear to be up at least 25% on a like-for-like basis from 2Q to 3Q.

This is above both Morgan Stanley’s and third-party estimates.

Moore also noted that longer-term concerns that the memory shortage will intensify in 2027 and again in 2028 remain as strong as ever. Morgan Stanley added that there is not enough memory relative to AI requirements and that it does not see this situation changing.

Notable quotes:

“Cloud customers are paying premiums to the expected 2Q price for six-week expedites; do we think those customers are paying those premia to stockpile memory in a warehouse?”

“AI is consuming so much DRAM that there isn’t enough left over for other sectors, and everywhere we look, we see indications that it is a true bottleneck. It’s holding back PC builds and smartphone builds.”

“Memory is not just constrained by AI demand—memory is increasingly one of the major primary constraints on AI demand, along with space and power.”

 $MU   $SKHY   $SNDK 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: “Nvidia’s CMX is equipped with 576 SSDs, providing a total storage capacity of 9,600 TB. Industry estimates suggest that NAND demand for CMX will surge from 35 million TB this year to more than 100 million TB next year.” Nvidia’s Rubin CMX will quite literally soak up NAND supply like a sponge absorbs water. To put 100 million TB into perspective, it is roughly equivalent to adding another Apple-sized source of demand to the NAND market.
items.content:
“Nvidia’s CMX is equipped with 576 SSDs, providing a total storage capacity of 9,600 TB. Industry estimates suggest that NAND demand for CMX will surge from 35 million TB this year to more than 100 million TB next year.”

Nvidia’s Rubin CMX will quite literally soak up NAND supply like a sponge absorbs water. To put 100 million TB into perspective, it is roughly equivalent to adding another Apple-sized source of demand to the NAND market.Jukan (@jukan05)Samsung–Nvidia Expand NAND Alliance as V10 Shipments Begin

Samsung Electronics is expanding its alliance with Nvidia into NAND flash supply, building on existing partnerships in DRAM, high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and foundry services. Leveraging its overwhelming production capacity, Samsung is increasing supplies of its flagship ninth-generation V-NAND (V9) to Nvidia while beginning mass production and shipments of its tenth-generation V-NAND (V10). The company is also accelerating the development of its 500-layer-class eleventh-generation V-NAND (V11).

According to industry sources on July 20, Samsung Electronics has established monthly V-NAND production capacity exceeding 100,000 wafers and is mass-producing and supplying V10 products to Nvidia.

Samsung has allocated approximately 60% of its V-NAND capacity to V9, elevating it to the company’s main product. Just a year ago, V8 accounted for a significant share of V-NAND output. Its share has since fallen below 40%, as Samsung rapidly shifted production toward V9 lines in response to surging demand for AI-server NAND from Nvidia and other global technology giants.

One reason Samsung is accelerating the expansion of its V9 capacity is Nvidia’s next-generation storage system, Context Memory Storage (CMX), which is scheduled for release in the second half of the year. Data centers are seeing a sharp increase in “key-value cache,” or KV cache, the contextual data generated during AI inference. KV cache has traditionally been handled by GPU servers, but as data volumes grow, dedicated storage systems equipped with solid-state drives (SSDs) offering capacities in the thousands of terabytes have become essential.

Nvidia’s CMX contains 576 SSDs and provides a total storage capacity of 9,600 TB. The industry expects NAND demand associated with CMX to surge from 35 million TB this year to more than 100 million TB next year. Samsung’s NAND output is projected to reach approximately 250 million TB this year. The company therefore intends to use its world-leading production capacity to establish itself as a key supplier for Nvidia’s CMX.

Samsung’s V9 NAND yield has reportedly risen above 80%, bringing production into a stable mass-manufacturing phase and providing a foundation for continued V-NAND supplies to Nvidia over the medium and long term. More than half of the NAND-dedicated cleanroom space at Samsung’s Pyeongtaek Plant 4 (P4) also remains available, allowing the company to expand V9 production lines as demand increases.

Samsung is also accelerating the mass production of next-generation V-NAND products. The company began full-scale V10 production in the first half of this year and has raised its share to just under 5% of total V-NAND output. V10 is a 400-layer-class product whose storage density is more than 50% higher than that of the 286-layer V9.

Trial production of the 500-layer-class V11 has also begun. V11 is an advanced, high-stack V-NAND product being developed with a target of at least 400 layers and potentially as many as 500. Trial production started early this year, and Samsung plans to double the relevant volume in the second half. By expanding trial output, the company aims to secure sufficient yield data and rapidly establish a mass-production system.

Samsung is pursuing materials innovation in parallel. Beginning with V10 NAND, the company will become the first in the industry to commercialize molybdenum, sometimes described as a “dream material.” Molybdenum can reduce wiring thickness by 30–40% compared with tungsten, the material currently used. Samsung partially adopted molybdenum in V9, which was released two years ago, but plans to expand its use substantially in V10.

Samsung Electronics Chairman Lee Jae-yong is expected to meet Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang in San Francisco soon to discuss expanding their cooperation. Meeting Huang again after nine months, Lee is reportedly set to discuss not only the supply of HBM and next-generation NAND products but also potential cooperation on data centers planned for construction in South Korea, including in Gwangju.

“Nvidia has asked Samsung to expand NAND production ahead of the CMX launch,” an industry official said. “Because the Samsung–Nvidia alliance spans the entire memory and foundry business, cooperation between Chairman Lee and CEO Huang is expected to deepen further.”—  https://x.com/jukan05/status/2079127696827879509 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

items.content:
Samsung–Nvidia Expand NAND Alliance as V10 Shipments Begin

Samsung Electronics is expanding its alliance with Nvidia into NAND flash supply, building on existing partnerships in DRAM, high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and foundry services. Leveraging its overwhelming production capacity, Samsung is increasing supplies of its flagship ninth-generation V-NAND (V9) to Nvidia while beginning mass production and shipments of its tenth-generation V-NAND (V10). The company is also accelerating the development of its 500-layer-class eleventh-generation V-NAND (V11).

According to industry sources on July 20, Samsung Electronics has established monthly V-NAND production capacity exceeding 100,000 wafers and is mass-producing and supplying V10 products to Nvidia.

Samsung has allocated approximately 60% of its V-NAND capacity to V9, elevating it to the company’s main product. Just a year ago, V8 accounted for a significant share of V-NAND output. Its share has since fallen below 40%, as Samsung rapidly shifted production toward V9 lines in response to surging demand for AI-server NAND from Nvidia and other global technology giants.

One reason Samsung is accelerating the expansion of its V9 capacity is Nvidia’s next-generation storage system, Context Memory Storage (CMX), which is scheduled for release in the second half of the year. Data centers are seeing a sharp increase in “key-value cache,” or KV cache, the contextual data generated during AI inference. KV cache has traditionally been handled by GPU servers, but as data volumes grow, dedicated storage systems equipped with solid-state drives (SSDs) offering capacities in the thousands of terabytes have become essential.

Nvidia’s CMX contains 576 SSDs and provides a total storage capacity of 9,600 TB. The industry expects NAND demand associated with CMX to surge from 35 million TB this year to more than 100 million TB next year. Samsung’s NAND output is projected to reach approximately 250 million TB this year. The company therefore intends to use its world-leading production capacity to establish itself as a key supplier for Nvidia’s CMX.

Samsung’s V9 NAND yield has reportedly risen above 80%, bringing production into a stable mass-manufacturing phase and providing a foundation for continued V-NAND supplies to Nvidia over the medium and long term. More than half of the NAND-dedicated cleanroom space at Samsung’s Pyeongtaek Plant 4 (P4) also remains available, allowing the company to expand V9 production lines as demand increases.

Samsung is also accelerating the mass production of next-generation V-NAND products. The company began full-scale V10 production in the first half of this year and has raised its share to just under 5% of total V-NAND output. V10 is a 400-layer-class product whose storage density is more than 50% higher than that of the 286-layer V9.

Trial production of the 500-layer-class V11 has also begun. V11 is an advanced, high-stack V-NAND product being developed with a target of at least 400 layers and potentially as many as 500. Trial production started early this year, and Samsung plans to double the relevant volume in the second half. By expanding trial output, the company aims to secure sufficient yield data and rapidly establish a mass-production system.

Samsung is pursuing materials innovation in parallel. Beginning with V10 NAND, the company will become the first in the industry to commercialize molybdenum, sometimes described as a “dream material.” Molybdenum can reduce wiring thickness by 30–40% compared with tungsten, the material currently used. Samsung partially adopted molybdenum in V9, which was released two years ago, but plans to expand its use substantially in V10.

Samsung Electronics Chairman Lee Jae-yong is expected to meet Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang in San Francisco soon to discuss expanding their cooperation. Meeting Huang again after nine months, Lee is reportedly set to discuss not only the supply of HBM and next-generation NAND products but also potential cooperation on data centers planned for construction in South Korea, including in Gwangju.

“Nvidia has asked Samsung to expand NAND production ahead of the CMX launch,” an industry official said. “Because the Samsung–Nvidia alliance spans the entire memory and foundry business, cooperation between Chairman Lee and CEO Huang is expected to deepen further.”
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: One development worth watching: the memory Big Three have halted the development of their proprietary CXL controllers. Their plans have reportedly been scrapped altogether. Their customers—the hyperscalers—were not enthusiastic about that model. What they wanted was the flexibility to pair a high-quality CXL controller with virtually any off-
items.content:
One development worth watching: the memory Big Three have halted the development of their proprietary CXL controllers. Their plans have reportedly been scrapped altogether.

Is this bearish news for CXL? Not at all. Let’s take a closer look.

To understand why the memory Big Three stopped developing their own CXL controllers, we first need to understand why they started in the first place.

According to Korean media reports, the three companies wanted to sell CXL controllers bundled with DRAM as high-margin finished products.

Their customers—the hyperscalers—were not enthusiastic about that model. What they wanted was the flexibility to pair a high-quality CXL controller with virtually any off-the-shelf DRAM available on the market.

In other words, developing expensive proprietary CXL controllers would offer little differentiation. The memory Big Three had approached CXL as an opportunity to create a new high-margin product similar to HBM. But if they could not sell the controller and DRAM together as a complete product, they would not be able to capture those high margins.

So, looking at it from a different angle, who stands to benefit?

The fabless companies that design CXL controllers.

With the memory Big Three—their largest potential competitors—abandoning in-house development, the CXL controller market is now effectively wide open.

This is very positive news for Montage and Marvell.Jukan (@jukan05)Memory Big Three Scrap In-House CXL Controller Chip Development

The world’s three largest memory chipmakers have all halted in-house development of controllers for Compute Express Link (CXL), a next-generation semiconductor technology in which they had invested heavily. The decision reflects concerns over a cannibalization dilemma: aggressively pushing proprietary chips into the market could undermine demand for commodity DRAM modules, or DIMMs, their core source of revenue. The memory giants are rapidly shifting to an outsourcing model, adopting chips designed by specialized fabless companies instead.

According to semiconductor industry sources on the 20th, Samsung Electronics, SK hynix, and Micron have scaled back or scrapped plans to commercialize controllers for CXL expansion devices. Fabless companies such as Montage Technology, Astera Labs, and PrimeMass are moving in to fill the gap.

Micron and SK Throw in the Towel, While Samsung Tests the Waters

Micron was the first to abandon in-house development. The U.S. chipmaker shut down its proprietary controller R&D operation and adopted a solution from PrimeMass. PrimeMass solutions now also appear in Micron’s product catalog.

SK hynix has likewise formally notified key partners that it is discontinuing its in-house CXL controller development project. The company is reassigning relevant personnel to processing-in-memory (PIM), a next-generation computational memory technology. The move reflects its intention to concentrate limited R&D resources on a more certain source of future growth.

Samsung Electronics is using its internally developed CXL controller only for research within the development team. The team is focusing on unproven areas, including CXL solutions based on low-power DRAM, or LPDDR. For products sold externally, Samsung is reportedly purchasing controllers from fabless suppliers.

This marks a departure from Samsung’s original plan. The company had initially intended to launch modules combining its proprietary controller with a CXL Memory Module, or CMM, but has since removed the formal commercialization of its in-house controller from its official roadmap.

“Samsung’s internal product-planning division has removed the in-house controller from its formal commercialization projects and retained it solely as an advanced R&D initiative,” said a semiconductor industry source familiar with the matter. “The development team is now concentrating on areas with uncertain near-term commercial viability, such as running mobile DRAM, or LPDDR, over CXL. In effect, Samsung has returned to a market-exploration phase.”

“Promoting CXL Could Destabilize DRAM”—The Cannibalization Dilemma

According to industry sources, all three memory manufacturers originally envisioned selling integrated CXL modules similar to Samsung’s proposed design. Their plan was to combine proprietary controller chips and DRAM on a single board and sell the resulting system as a high-priced finished product.

Major data-center customers, however, wanted something different. To reduce the enormous cost of building infrastructure, they preferred a disaggregated architecture: installing the CXL controller separately on the system motherboard and inserting widely available, inexpensive commodity DRAM modules into slots behind it for reuse.

Analysts say this is where the business models came into conflict and why the manufacturers changed course. If memory companies insisted on expensive integrated products built around proprietary controllers that had required substantial development spending, cost-conscious customers would be reluctant to buy them. Worse still, such products could reduce demand for the commodity DRAM modules that the companies already sell in large volumes. In other words, aggressively promoting finished CXL products could cannibalize the DIMM market that serves as their largest source of revenue.

“If a memory manufacturer’s finished CXL expansion product must compete directly with its own core cash cow—DIMMs—it becomes difficult to pursue the business aggressively,” said a semiconductor industry official. “Management concluded that there was little practical benefit in forcing the commercialization of in-house controllers while risking a conflict with the company’s existing core business.”

Experts stress that the three companies’ decisions should not be interpreted as a retreat from or abandonment of CXL technology and the CXL market itself. Rather, they see the move as a way to reduce risk during the market’s uncertain early stages and return to the semiconductor industry’s naturally efficient division of labor.

“Instead of trying to monopolize control of CXL, the memory manufacturers have opted for a pragmatic division of labor with fabless companies,” another industry official said. “As the global semiconductor market becomes more sophisticated, this optimized ecosystem—in which specialized fabless companies handle design and memory manufacturers focus on production—is expected to accelerate further.”—  https://x.com/jukan05/status/2079103447157338422 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: I have no idea what this Chinese account’s agenda is in spreading this kind of FUD. It is not true that memory module prices are falling or that memory prices will soon be down 50–70% for the year. Do not read or trust Chinese accounts like this.

items.title: Meritz Securities’ comments on the surge in server DRAM (64GB) spot prices: - This is the key chart from yesterday’s report, illustrating how to interpret the gap between spot and contract prices. - Server DRAM spot prices have risen sharply since mid-July. Unlike conventional DRAM products, server DRAM spot prices are not tracked by platforms such as DRAMeXchange. - Demand from sovereign AI projects, including those in Saudi Arabia, as well as testing demand from new AI data centers, has increased significantly. Supply has become so tight that vendors are reportedly struggling to secure enough units even for customer samples. - As a result, spot prices have surged above $3,100, representing
items.content:
Meritz Securities’ comments on the surge in server DRAM (64GB) spot prices:

- This is the key chart from yesterday’s report, illustrating how to interpret the gap between spot and contract prices.

- Server DRAM spot prices have risen sharply since mid-July. Unlike conventional DRAM products, server DRAM spot prices are not tracked by platforms such as DRAMeXchange.

- Demand from sovereign AI projects, including those in Saudi Arabia, as well as testing demand from new AI data centers, has increased significantly. Supply has become so tight that vendors are reportedly struggling to secure enough units even for customer samples.

- As a result, spot prices have surged above $3,100, representing a 146% premium to the end-June contract price of $1,380.

- The current situation resembles what happened at the beginning of this year. In January, a sudden wave of rush orders from the server market drove spot prices sharply higher. However, memory stocks remained range-bound as the market waited for hyperscalers’ month-end earnings announcements.

- Memory stocks subsequently rebounded from February as contract prices began to rise. This time as well, the market is likely to focus on—and respond to—upward revisions in contract prices following hyperscalers’ earnings announcements.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 20, 2026

items.title: FOXCONN HAS BROKEN DELL AND SUPERMICRO’S DUOPOLY BY WINNING ITS FIRST CONTRACT TO MANUFACTURE SPACEX’S NEXT-GENERATION AI SERVERS POWERED BY NVIDIA’S GB300. - The project is reportedly worth approximately $52 billion, making it a massive deal. - This order was not included in the company’s existing revenue guidance, suggesting that Hon Hai’s AI business momentum could be far stronger than previously expected. Combined with strong results from its subsidiary FII, expectations for Hon Hai’s second-half performance have risen significantly.
items.content:
FOXCONN HAS BROKEN DELL AND SUPERMICRO’S DUOPOLY BY WINNING ITS FIRST CONTRACT TO MANUFACTURE SPACEX’S NEXT-GENERATION AI SERVERS POWERED BY NVIDIA’S GB300.

- The project is reportedly worth approximately $52 billion, making it a massive deal.

- This order was not included in the company’s existing revenue guidance, suggesting that Hon Hai’s AI business momentum could be far stronger than previously expected. Combined with strong results from its subsidiary FII, expectations for Hon Hai’s second-half performance have risen significantly.
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 19, 2026

items.title: Who said Kimi K3 is bearish for compute? Lol.
items.content:
Who said Kimi K3 is bearish for compute? Lol.Kimi.ai (@Kimi_Moonshot)Kimi K3 has received far more love than we expected, and our GPUs are feeling it.

Over the past 48 hours, demand has pushed close to the limits of our current capacity. To protect the experience of existing subscribers, we're temporarily pausing new subscriptions and prioritizing compute for current members. Existing subscribed users are not affected.

We're adding capacity as fast as we can and will reopen new subscription spots in batches.

Going forward, we'll also split membership into two more focused plans: Kimi Membership for Kimi Web, App, and Work; and Kimi Code Membership for coding workflows. This will help us match compute more precisely and keep the experience stable.

Thank you for your patience and understanding!—  https://x.com/Kimi_Moonshot/status/2078855608565207130 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 19, 2026

This will help us match compute more precisely and keep the
items.content:
Kimi K3 has received far more love than we expected, and our GPUs are feeling it.

Over the past 48 hours, demand has pushed close to the limits of our current capacity. To protect the experience of existing subscribers, we're temporarily pausing new subscriptions and prioritizing compute for current members. Existing subscribed users are not affected.

We're adding capacity as fast as we can and will reopen new subscription spots in batches.

Going forward, we'll also split membership into two more focused plans: Kimi Membership for Kimi Web, App, and Work; and Kimi Code Membership for coding workflows. This will help us match compute more precisely and keep the experience stable.

Thank you for your patience and understanding!
— @Kimi_Moonshot Jul 19, 2026

items.title: After reading @deanwball’s piece, I had the opportunity to read several expert-call transcripts. Having done so, I concluded that his argument is, to some extent, mistaken. Here is why. Take DeepSeek as an example. Even though DeepSeek has open-sourced its model weights and parts of its software architecture, competitors would still find it difficult to replicate its cost advantage. That is because, while DeepSeek has disclosed most of its model architecture, the critical implementation details and operational know-how remain proprietary. items.content:
After reading  @deanwball ’s piece, I had the opportunity to read several expert-call transcripts. Having done so, I concluded that his argument is, to some extent, mistaken.

Here is why.

Take DeepSeek as an example.

Even though DeepSeek has open-sourced its model weights and parts of its software architecture, competitors would still find it difficult to replicate its cost advantage.

That is because, while DeepSeek has disclosed most of its model architecture, the critical implementation details and operational know-how remain proprietary.

As a result, even if Chinese or U.S. hyperscalers deploy DeepSeek’s open-source models on identical hardware, DeepSeek’s own deployment environment can achieve—and is already achieving—greater operational efficiency and a lower average inference cost.

This efficiency advantage allows DeepSeek to price tokens through its official API below third-party platforms while still maintaining an API margin of 70%.

Ultimately, China’s decision to release model weights cannot simply be characterized as dumping. Chinese companies may lack sufficient compute capacity to serve all the inference demand themselves, but they are not selling at a loss or failing to recoup their training costs.Haseeb ＞|＜ (@hosseeb)This argument by  @deanwball is being badly misunderstood. It's OK to disagree with it, but first you have to actually understand what he's saying.

He's saying: releasing the weights for a frontier-level model is effectively dumping.

Dumping is when you sell a product at significantly below cost in order to corner market share. It's illegal. The reason: dumping results in short-term consumer surplus, but long-term it prevents the formation of a competitive market and discourages capex outside of the dumper. Standard Oil famously did this in order to consolidate the oil market before it was broken up.

So why is he claiming releasing the weights of a frontier level model is basically dumping? Isn't he just describing open source?

His argument: it's not financially sustainable to train a frontier model and release the weights. In the long run, you will not be able to internalize enough of the gains given the cost of training a frontier model, because neoclouds and other inference providers will be able to outcompete you at actually serving the model. It costs an astronomical amount of money to train frontier models, and if everyone else can serve them, you don't capture enough of the surplus to pay for the training and R&D.

It's not like normal open source when you build some software and then release it and sell services on top of it. The amount of capex required for frontier-level models is an order of magnitude higher than normal software, which is why doing this at frontier level is so economically irrational. 

Right now the Hong Kong stock market is ebullient enough that Chinese AI companies are not getting punished for the fact that they're all deeply, deeply unprofitable. Releasing model weights is great marketing, intellectually appealing, and strikes fear into the hearts of their opponents. We can assume the status quo continues for a while because of the AI supercycle. But eventually the AI market will correct, the Hong Kong market will dump, and suddenly these Chinese labs won't be able to afford to training super expensive models without internalizing more of the gains.

But what if China, seeing that this strategy is successfully kneecapping the US lead (by discouraging further capex and lowering valuations), says no--don't stop. And so the Chinese government starts buying up the shares of these companies and demanding that they continue releasing frontier-level weights, profitable or not. 

In that case, it becomes a genuine space race. For-profit companies cannot continue to compete on either side. US labs valuations fall, and the White House realizes that to keep their advantage in the AI race, they cannot rely on the free market to maintain their lead. They nationalize the labs and fund them off government subsidies.

Now you have government-controlled and distributed models on both sides. That's what Dean is calling the "dystopian hellscape."

The best analogy is drug development: if China were to sell American drugs back to us really cheaply, that would result in a large short-term consumer surplus. Cheap Viagra and Ozempic is obviously great. But in the long run, this would discourage investment in developing new drugs. That's the sense that Dean is saying it's long-term "decel."

Now, I happen to disagree with Dean. I think the consumer surplus of having frontier-level open weight models is huge, even at the current capabilities. I also think China is going to defect from this strategy soon (there's been reporting along these lines, that Beijing will stop allowing large models to be open-weight; I think there are other reasons for this aside from competition). I also suspect that nationalization of labs is inevitable as they take on more geopolitical and cyber capabilities. 

But he's not wrong--releasing frontier-level weight models is weird.

The question of how long this market will remain profit-driven is a very coherent question to ask.—  https://x.com/hosseeb/status/2078614672086036555 
— Jukan (@jukan05) Jul 19, 2026

items.title: As an analyst and writer, nothing thrills me more than seeing an argument I made before anyone else gain recognition and acceptance—and then hearing others make the same or similar points. I’m absolutely thrilled right now.
